content.text: Execution sandboxing confines the runtime environment in which agent-generated code runs so that even if that code is malicious or erroneous, it cannot affect the host system, neighboring processes, or external networks. The sandbox is the hardware-enforced or kernel-enforced perimeter that makes agent code execution safe.

An execution sandbox is an isolated runtime environment with a restricted view of the operating system. Code running inside the sandbox can compute and produce output, but its ability to read the filesystem, make network connections, spawn new processes, or consume unbounded resources is controlled by layers of enforcement outside its own control.

For AI agents that generate and execute code — such as data-analysis agents, code-interpreter assistants, or self-modifying planning agents — the sandbox is the difference between a feature and a critical vulnerability. Without it, a single malformed model output can compromise the host.

LLMs produce plausible-looking code that may be wrong in subtle ways. In a pure text generation context, a hallucinated function name is harmless — the code is never executed. But agents with code-execution tools run that code. A prompt-injection attack that causes the model to generate
is catastrophic without a sandbox and is merely a failed tool call with one.

Sandboxing also matters for accidental damage: a buggy loop that exhausts RAM, a recursive function that consumes all CPU, or a careless file write that corrupts shared state. Resource limits inside the sandbox contain all of these to a single isolated environment.
— agent code cannot read host files, access credentials, or communicate with internal services.
— even if the model is tricked into generating malicious code, the sandbox absorbs the impact.
— a clean ephemeral sandbox per execution eliminates state leakage between agent runs.
— cgroup limits prevent one agent execution from starving others in a multi-tenant system.
— multiple independent enforcement layers mean a bypass at one level is still blocked by the next.
— spinning up a fresh container or microVM adds 200ms–2s per execution for strong isolation approaches.
— blocked syscalls and network access mean legitimate code that requires them will fail inside the sandbox.
— correctly configuring seccomp profiles, capability sets, cgroup limits, and read-only filesystems requires deep systems knowledge.
— user-space-only sandboxes (eval restrictions, AST filters) are breakable; real isolation requires kernel-level enforcement.
Execution sandboxing works through a stack of independent enforcement mechanisms. Each layer compensates for weaknesses in the others — this is defence in depth.

Linux namespaces partition the kernel's view of resources. A container has its own PID namespace (cannot see host processes), network namespace (isolated network stack), mount namespace (isolated filesystem), and UTS namespace (isolated hostname). This is the foundation that Docker, Podman, and Kubernetes build on.
Linux capabilities split root's monolithic privilege into fine-grained tokens. Dropping all capabilities at container startup (
) prevents privilege escalation. Seccomp-BPF further restricts which syscalls are allowed; a strict profile allows only
, and a small set of memory management calls.

Control groups (cgroups) enforce resource quotas independently of whether the sandboxed code tries to claim more. CPU quota, memory limit, and PID count are all enforced at the kernel level — the code inside cannot escape them regardless of what syscalls it makes.

Tools like Firecracker spin up a KVM-based microVM in ~125ms. The guest kernel is fully isolated from the host; even a kernel exploit in the sandbox cannot reach the host kernel without breaking the hypervisor. This is the strongest isolation available for untrusted code execution.

attrs.alt: Execution sandbox isolation layers from host OS through container, seccomp, cgroup, and user-space

content.text: Each concentric boundary represents an independent enforcement layer. Code running in the innermost user-space sandbox must bypass all outer layers to reach the host — each layer independently prevents the most common escape vectors.
Any agent with a code-execution tool (Python interpreter, shell, JavaScript runtime) must sandbox — no exceptions.
Agents that process untrusted content (user-uploaded files, web-scraped data, third-party API responses) should execute any parsing or transformation inside a sandbox.
Multi-tenant platforms where one user's agent must not affect another user's data or performance.
Any agentic system where the risk model cannot guarantee the model never produces malicious code.

import subprocess
import tempfile
import os

def execute_in_sandbox(code: str, timeout: int = 30) -> dict:
    """
    Run arbitrary Python code in a Docker container with strict isolation.
    Returns {stdout, stderr, exit_code}.
    """
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
        mode='w', suffix='.py', delete=False, prefix='/tmp/agent_code_'
    ) as f:
        f.write(code)
        code_path = f.name

    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            [
                "docker", "run",
                "--rm",                         # remove on exit
                "--network", "none",            # no network access
                "--read-only",                  # immutable rootfs
                "--tmpfs", "/tmp:size=32m",     # writable scratch space
                "--memory", "256m",             # RAM limit
                "--memory-swap", "256m",        # disable swap
                "--cpus", "0.5",               # CPU limit
                "--pids-limit", "64",           # fork-bomb protection
                "--cap-drop", "ALL",            # drop all capabilities
                "--security-opt", "no-new-privileges",
                "--security-opt", "seccomp=/etc/docker/seccomp/agent.json",
                "-v", f"{code_path}:/code/script.py:ro",
                "python:3.12-slim",
                "python", "/code/script.py",
            ],
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
            timeout=timeout,
        )
        return {
            "stdout": result.stdout[:50_000],   # cap output size
            "stderr": result.stderr[:10_000],
            "exit_code": result.returncode,
        }
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
        return {"stdout": "", "stderr": "Execution timed out", "exit_code": -1}
    finally:
        os.unlink(code_path)

For low-latency requirements, a user-space filter like RestrictedPython provides a lightweight first layer. It rewrites Python ASTs to block dangerous attribute access — but it should always be paired with container isolation, not used alone.

# Firecracker requires a KVM host. This shows the API call structure.
# In practice use the Python SDK or a platform like Fly.io / Modal
curl -X PUT 'http://localhost:8080/machine-config' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "vcpu_count": 1,
    "mem_size_mib": 256,
    "smt": false
  }'

# Start the microVM
curl -X PUT 'http://localhost:8080/actions' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"action_type": "InstanceStart"}'

# Code executes inside the guest kernel — host kernel never touched
# Guest terminates after execution; VM is discarded

OpenAI's Code Interpreter (ChatGPT's data analysis feature) runs Python in an isolated container per session. When a user asks "analyze this CSV", the model generates Python code; the code runs in a Firecracker microVM or similar; only the text output and generated images return to the host. The model never has access to the host filesystem, the user's other sessions, or the internet.

E2B (a code sandbox service) offers an API-driven approach used by many agent frameworks. A single API call spins up a Firecracker microVM with a pre-warmed Python environment, executes code with a 30s timeout, and returns stdout. Cold-start is under 500ms using VM snapshots.
— blocking 'import os' in Python does not prevent os access through object graph traversal or C extensions. Always combine with container-level isolation.
Running as root inside the container
— a container escape becomes a host root compromise. Always run as a non-root user inside the sandbox.
— a long-running container accumulates state. Use ephemeral containers (--rm) or snapshot/restore per execution.
— agent code that generates gigabytes of stdout can OOM the reader process. Cap output size at the subprocess level.
— bind-mounting an API key file or environment variables with credentials gives sandbox code access to those secrets.
Use the principle of least privilege: give the sandbox only what the code actually needs, nothing more.
Ephemeral by default: destroy the container after each execution. State that persists across executions is a security liability.
Layer defenses: combine user-space restrictions, namespace isolation, seccomp filtering, and resource limits — each addresses different attack vectors.
Use pre-warmed snapshots for low-latency requirements. Firecracker supports snapshotting a booted VM so cold starts are restored, not re-booted.
Never mount the host Docker socket into a sandbox — this gives container-level code full control over the host.

The performance cost of sandboxing depends on isolation depth. A pre-warmed Docker container can start in under 50ms. Firecracker microVMs with snapshots achieve ~125ms cold starts and near-zero resume times. These numbers are acceptable for interactive agent workflows.
Pool pre-warmed containers or microVMs to eliminate cold-start latency for common use cases.
Set hard execution timeouts (30s–120s) to prevent runaway code from consuming pool capacity.
Monitor sandbox escape attempts via kernel audit logs or eBPF probes — anomalous syscall patterns reveal attack attempts.
— managed code sandbox service with Python/JS runtimes, file upload, and a Python/JS SDK. Optimized for agent code execution.
— AWS open-source microVM hypervisor; powers Lambda and Fargate. Best-in-class isolation for untrusted code.
— Google's user-space kernel that intercepts syscalls before they reach the host; used in GKE Sandbox.
— Zope's AST-rewriting library for user-space Python restrictions.
— serverless platforms that spin up Firecracker microVMs on demand, billing per-second.

The security of a sandbox is only as strong as its weakest layer. Container escapes (CVE-level vulnerabilities in runc and containerd) have occurred historically. Defense-in-depth with seccomp, capability dropping, non-root users, and read-only filesystems makes exploiting a container escape significantly harder even if a vulnerability exists.
Keep container runtimes patched. Most container escapes are fixed within days; unpatched runtimes are the primary risk.
Use read-only root filesystems and tmpfs scratch mounts to prevent persistence of attacker-written files.
Monitor for anomalous syscall patterns using Falco or eBPF probes — these detect attacks that bypass container walls but leave kernel footprints.
Any agent that executes generated code must run it in an isolated sandbox — no exceptions.
Effective sandboxing requires multiple independent layers: namespace isolation, capability/seccomp filtering, cgroup limits, and optionally microVM hypervisor isolation.
User-space filters (AST rewriting) are a useful first layer but are breakable on their own — always combine with container-level enforcement.
Ephemeral containers destroyed after each execution prevent state accumulation and lateral movement.
Firecracker microVMs provide hypervisor-level isolation at sub-second cold-start times, making them viable for interactive agent workflows.
Never mount secrets, the host Docker socket, or host directories into the sandbox.
Keep runtimes patched and monitor for anomalous syscall patterns — defense in depth buys time even when vulnerabilities exist.
